title: Login Screen
description: Learn print(), input(), variables, and if/elif/else selection. Build a working login screen with a provided password validator.

steps.title: print()
steps.content:
Displaying Output with print()

print() is the function that sends text to the screen. Anything you pass inside the brackets gets displayed.

print("Welcome to the system")

You can print any kind of value — text (wrapped in quotes), numbers, or the result of an expression:

print("Hello")
print(42)
print(10 + 5)

Run the editor below and try changing the values:

steps.title: Multiple Arguments
steps.content:
Printing Multiple Values

Pass several values separated by commas and print() will display them all on one line with a space in between each one automatically.

print("User:", "alice")
print("Attempts remaining:", 3)
print("Status:", "active", "| Role:", "admin")
User: alice Attempts remaining: 3 Status: active | Role: admin
Why commas? Commas let you mix text and numbers without any extra work — Python converts numbers to text for you. This is the simplest way to build an output line from multiple pieces.

Experiment — try mixing your own labels and values:

steps.title: Variables
steps.content:
Storing Values in Variables

A variable is a named container that holds a value. You create one with the = sign — this is called assignment.

username = "alice"
role = "admin"
login_attempts = 0

print("User:", username)
print("Role:", role)
print("Attempts:", login_attempts)
Naming rules: variable names can contain letters, numbers and underscores, but cannot start with a number or contain spaces. Use snake_case by convention.

Variables can be updated at any point — the old value is simply replaced:

login_attempts = 0
print("Before:", login_attempts)
login_attempts = 1
print("After:", login_attempts)
steps.js:
PyLearn.createEditor({ containerId: 'editor-variables', initialCode: 'username = "alice"
role = "admin"
login_attempts = 0

print("User:", username)
print("Role:", role)
print("Attempts:", login_attempts)

login_attempts = 1
print("Updated attempts:", login_attempts)', label: 'Variables' });

steps.title: input()
steps.content:
Getting Input from the User

input() pauses the program and waits for the user to type something and press Enter. Whatever they type is returned as a string.

name = input("Enter your name: ")
print("Hello,", name)

The text inside the brackets is the prompt — it's shown to the user so they know what to type. You always store the result in a variable.

Important: input() always returns a string, even if the user types a number. You would need int(input(...)) to get an integer — but for usernames and passwords that is never needed.

In the editor below a simulated input is provided. Try changing the prompt text or the variable name:

💡 Skulpt simulates input() — in a real terminal the user would type their response.

steps.title: Storing Input
steps.content:
Asking for Multiple Inputs

You can call input() as many times as you need. Each call waits for the user to respond before moving on.

username = input("Username: ")
password = input("Password: ")

print("Attempting login for:", username)

Notice we store each answer in its own variable. This is the pattern every login screen uses — ask for username first, then password.

Security note: in a real application you would never print the password back to the screen. We do it here only to see what was captured.

💡 Simulated inputs: alice then secret123

steps.title: if Statements
steps.content:
Making Decisions with if

An if statement runs a block of code only when a condition is True. The indented lines beneath it are only executed if the condition holds.

username = "alice"

if username == "alice":
    print("Known user found")
    print("Proceeding...")

Note the double equals == for comparison — a single = is for assignment, == is for checking equality.

OperatorMeaningExample
==Equal tousername == "alice"
!=Not equal tostatus != "blocked"
>Greater thanattempts > 3
<Less thanattempts < 3

steps.title: if / else
steps.content:
if / else

Add an else block to handle the case when the condition is False. Exactly one of the two branches will always run.

username = "bob"

if username == "alice":
    print("Access granted")
else:
    print("Access denied")

This is the core of a login check — either the credentials match or they don't. Try swapping the username to "alice" and re-running:

steps.title: if / elif / else
steps.content:
elif — Extra Branches

elif (short for "else if") adds more conditions between if and else. Python checks them top to bottom and runs only the first one that matches.

username = "admin"

if username == "alice":
    print("Hello, Alice")
elif username == "admin":
    print("Hello, Administrator")
elif username == "guest":
    print("Limited access only")
else:
    print("Unknown user")
Key rule: as soon as one branch matches, Python skips all the rest. Only one branch ever runs per execution.

steps.title: Logical Operators
steps.content:
Combining Conditions: and, or, not

Real login checks involve more than one condition at the same time. Python has three logical operators for combining conditions:

OperatorMeaningTrue when…
andBoth conditionsusername correct AND password correct
orEither conditionusername is alice OR username is admin
notOppositeaccount is NOT blocked
username = "alice"
password = "correct"

if username == "alice" and password == "correct":
    print("Access granted")
else:
    print("Access denied")

steps.title: The Password Validator
steps.content:
A Pre-Built Password Validator

Writing a secure password-checking function is complex, so one has been provided for you. You don't need to write or understand the inside of it — only how to call it and use the result.

def check_password(username, password):
    """Returns True if password is correct for username."""
    accounts = {
        "alice":  "Sunshine7",
        "bob":    "Football3",
        "admin":  "Adm1n!Pass",
        "guest":  "guest",
    }
    if username not in accounts:
        return False
    return accounts[username] == password

🔒 This function is defined at the top of your program. You call it, read the result, and use if to decide what to print.

Calling the function returns True or False. You use that with an if statement:

result = check_password("alice", "Sunshine7")

if result == True:
    print("Password accepted")
else:
    print("Password rejected")
Valid accounts: alice / Sunshine7  ·  bob / Football3  ·  admin / Adm1n!Pass  ·  guest / guest

steps.title: Practice: Print a Receipt
steps.content:
Practice 1 — Print a Login Receipt

Using only commas (no formatting), print these three lines exactly. The values on the right come from the variables already defined.

User: alice Role: admin Attempts: 0

The variables username, role, and attempts are already defined — use them, don't type the values directly.

steps.title: Practice: Access Check
steps.content:
Practice 2 — Access Level Check

A variable role is set to one of three values. Use if / elif / else to print the correct message for each:

role valuePrint
"admin"Full access
"user"Standard access
anything elseNo access

Your code is tested with all three cases automatically.

steps.title: Practice: Ask and Greet
steps.content:
Practice 3 — Ask and Greet

Ask the user for their name with the prompt "Enter your name: ", store it, then print:

Welcome, Alice

The name comes from input() — use a comma in print() to join the label and the variable. The validator supplies Alice as the simulated input.

steps.title: Challenge: Login Screen
steps.content:
Final Challenge — Build the Login Screen

Put everything together. Your program must:

  1. Ask for a username with the prompt "Username: "
  2. Ask for a password with the prompt "Password: "
  3. Call check_password(username, password)
  4. If it returns True — print exactly: Access granted
  5. If it returns False — print exactly: Access denied
Valid credentials: alice / Sunshine7  ·  bob / Football3  ·  admin / Adm1n!Pass  ·  guest / guest
The validator tests your code with a correct login and a wrong one.

steps.content:
Endless Mode ∞
